rationale: The cited paper is used to establish a key prior finding in the feature-addition literature: the utility of added features is context dependent, including differences between pre-use and post-use evaluation. The citing paper explicitly says it is extending this line of research by testing whether utility/adoption effects also depend on innovation locus (core vs peripheral), not just on evaluation timing or other contexts. The citation is also revisited in the contribution section as part of the specific literature stream the paper positions itself against, indicating a meaningful framing role rather than a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on the Feature Fatigue stream by taking its context-dependent view of feature utility and extending it to a new contingency: where the innovation is located in the product system (core vs detachable peripheral). It argues that locus, especially for really new innovations, changes adoption through mechanisms beyond those emphasized in prior feature-addition work.
